g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Semaphores and Synchronization, a fundamental concept in operating systems.
g.text: Operating Systems - Critical Section Problem and Solutions

g.data-narration: In concurrent systems, multiple processes accessing shared resources can cause race conditions where the outcome depends on execution order.
g.text: When multiple processes access shared resources simultaneously,
the final outcome depends on the order of execution.
This is called a RACE CONDITION

g.data-narration: The critical section is code that accesses shared resources. We need mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.
g.text: Code segment where shared resources are accessed.
Only ONE process should execute in its critical section at a time.

g.data-narration: A semaphore is a synchronization variable introduced by Dijkstra with atomic wait() and signal() operations.
g.text: A synchronization tool introduced by Edsger Dijkstra.
Integer variable with two ATOMIC operations: wait() and signal()

g.data-narration: wait() decrements the semaphore and blocks if negative. signal() increments and wakes a waiting process.
Counting semaphores manage multiple resource instances. Binary semaphores (0 or 1) implement mutual exclusion locks.
Spinlocks use busy waiting (efficient for short waits). Blocking semaphores put processes to sleep, efficient for longer waits.
g.text: Loops until semaphore becomes positive. Wastes CPU but no context switch.
Process sleeps in queue, wakes on signal. Efficient for longer waits.

g.data-narration: Use a binary semaphore initialized to 1. wait() acquires the lock, signal() releases it - ensuring only one process enters.
Semaphores can enforce execution order. Initialize to 0, P1 signals after work, P2 waits before proceeding.
g.text: Enforce execution order between processes using semaphore initialized to 0.

g.data-narration: The bounded buffer problem uses three semaphores: mutex for mutual exclusion, empty for available slots, full for filled slots.
Multiple readers can access simultaneously, but writers need exclusive access. Uses read_count and wrt semaphores.
Deadlock occurs when processes wait indefinitely for each other. Avoid by acquiring locks in consistent global order.
Key takeaways: Semaphores provide atomic wait/signal, counting for resources, binary for locks, solve critical section problems.
Exam tips: Know initialization values, emphasize atomicity, understand classical problems, watch wait() order carefully.
g.text: Always state initialization values | Emphasize atomicity | Watch wait() order | Practice Producer-Consumer
